conventions: Mid-year discounting convention. Valuation only — no live market price is used.

drivers.label: Revenue growth (Yr 1–10)
drivers.comment: The earlier read treated Apple as a flat hardware franchise fading to ~3%. That under-modeled two things the market is capitalizing: Services compounding at low-double-digits (now a large, sticky share of the mix) and an AI-driven iPhone upgrade cycle refreshing a >1.5B-device installed base. Base now starts ~8% and fades to 4%; Bull runs a genuine supercycle (~12% → 5%); Bear still reflects saturation and China risk (~4% → 2%).

drivers.label: EBIT margin (Yr 1–10)
drivers.comment: This is the other half of what was missing. Services gross margins run ~70% versus ~37% for hardware, so as Services outgrows product the *blended* operating margin should climb — not stay pinned near 32%. Base expands toward 37% over the decade; Bull toward 40% if AI features accelerate the mix-shift. Defensible given the trajectory, though 40% would be an all-time high.

drivers.label: Capex (Yr 1–10, % rev)
drivers.comment: ~3% of revenue — Apple is asset-light and outsources manufacturing, a deliberate contrast to the AI-infra names (MSFT/GOOGL at 15–28%). Not a swing factor; the model barely moves on capex.

drivers.label: D&A (% of revenue)
drivers.comment: Small and stable (~2.8% of revenue), consistent with the light asset base. Not a swing factor.

drivers.label: Net working capital (% of revenue)
drivers.comment: Apple famously runs *negative* working capital — payables and deferred revenue exceed receivables and inventory — so incremental growth is partly self-funding (a source of cash). Modeled from a small drag in Bear (+1%) to a small source in Bull (−1%). A real advantage over most peers.

drivers.label: Net cash (cash + securities − debt)
drivers.verdict: Corrected → this was the model's clear error
drivers.comment: The previous input counted only $35.9B of cash and so treated Apple — the most cash-rich company on earth — as a NET BORROWER of ~$42B. It ignored Apple's ~$120B marketable-securities portfolio (current + long-term investments). Cash is now stated as cash + marketable securities (~$156.6B), leaving Apple net-cash by ~$78B. The fix adds the whole ~$120B swing (~$8/share) straight to equity value. Real, unambiguous, and it should never have been missing.

drivers.label: Tax rate
drivers.comment: 21% US statutory; Apple's effective rate has run ~15–17%, so this trims NOPAT a touch (biases value down).

drivers.label: Risk-free rate
drivers.comment: ≈ the 10-year Treasury yield. ~4.4–4.5% is consistent with current levels.

drivers.label: Equity risk premium
drivers.comment: 4.0–4.3% ≈ Damodaran's implied ERP of 4.23% (2026). This is where current market conditions legitimately enter the model.

drivers.label: Beta
drivers.comment: ~1.0–1.2; Apple carries slightly more market sensitivity than the flagship software names. Lower in the Bull case (a quality/safety bid compresses perceived risk).

drivers.label: WACC (derived)
drivers.verdict: Solid — and the swing factor vs the market
drivers.comment: CAPM build-up nets to ~8.3–9.5% across scenarios. A durable, cash-generative franchise like Apple arguably earns a lower discount rate than this; every 1% off WACC lifts the terminal value materially. If the market is applying a ~7% 'quality' discount rate, that alone explains a large part of the gap to a fundamentals DCF.

drivers.label: Terminal growth
drivers.verdict: Base OK · Bull at the ceiling
drivers.comment: Terminal g should not exceed the ~4% risk-free / long-run nominal GDP. Bull's 3.5% now sits right at that edge — appropriate only if you truly believe the AI/Services franchise still out-grows the economy in perpetuity.

drivers.label: Scenario probability
drivers.verdict: The market is pricing our Bull as its base case
drivers.comment: Weights are Bear 20% / Base 45% / Bull 35%. Read against the tape, the honest takeaway: today's market valuation sits up at our *Bull* scenario — i.e. the market effectively assigns near-100% to the AI-supercycle path (double-digit growth, 40% margins, a quality-level discount rate) that we give 35%. That is either correct (the AI upgrade cycle plus Services re-rates Apple structurally) or it is Apple priced for flawless execution with little margin of safety. Our weights deliberately keep real probability on the Base and Bear paths; the gap between our weighted value and the market IS the AI optimism, priced.
